segment-text: Sandra Berg & Judith Berg
A DARK SKY

Floodlights crisscross the screen. The letters KPIX flash in red neon. CAMERA TILTS DOWN from the letters to a radio tower sitting high above a squat building lined with palm trees. A banner is strung across the front of the radio station... WELCOME TO DOOLEY'S 1933 ANNUAL AMATEUR CONTEST It's Saturday night in Hollywood. Every Tom, Dick and Harry hurries toward KPIX. The building swarms with people battling to get in. OVER this we hear...
Hello, hello, hello again. Lefty McCoy here in Hollywood bringing you the last
hour of Dooley's Amateur Contest where
INT. KPIX - BACKSTAGE - NIGHT

A touch of Barnum & Bailey. rehearsing their acts: The final contestants each -- First, a Female Blues Singer squeezing into her sequined gown; -- Next, two Old Coots practicing a soft shoe; -- Then, a five-piece Girls' Band tuning up. CLOSEUP - PERFECTLY-MANICURED MAN'S HAND held out stiffly. A WOMAN'S HANDS slip a pair of white kid gloves on the man's hands. CAMERA EASES BACK to reveal HAMILTON BROOKS, dapper from his pin-striped suit to his polished gold walking stick. Definitely out of place in these surroundings. ROCHELLE ABERNATHY, a beautiful, brainless blonde, buttons Brooks' gloves. Rochelle is draped in a luxurious sable. Behind them, a Man covered head to toe in BELLS, shakes to the tune of "Goody Goody". CAMERA finally RESTS on an angeliclooking young girl, PRISCILLA SIMPSON, in top hat and cane. Her MOTHER hovers over her doing the usual stage-mother primping. Priscilla coyly reaches out to touch Rochelle's sable, but the tip of Brooks' gold cane shoves her hand away. Brooks' and Priscilla's eyes lock.
INT. KPIX, ON STAGE

The announcer, LEFTY McCOY, a flashy little squirt, stands next to a life-size cutout of another announcer, Dan Frye. Behind Lefty and the cardboard announcer sits an orchestra. jam-packed audience faces them.
A
The scratchy SOUNDS of a female trio BLARE from SPEAKERS behind the cutout. As the song ends, a scratchy VOICE booms out.

Lefty, that was the Carlino Sisters coming at you live from the Big Apple, where the mercury's dropped to a frosty 27 degrees. Oh, to be out there in sunny California, Lefty. LEFTY (to Dan's cutout) At least you've got the Carlino Sisters to keep you warm, Dan. Do you read me?
(barely audible) Loud and clear.

(to audience) That, of course, is Dan Frye, comin' to you live and direct from our sister station, WPIX-NEW YORK. Don't forget, all you out there in Radioland are the ones voting. There's less than an hour left to call the station in your city and tell us who you want for Dooley's Star of Tomorrow.
INT. KPIX, BACKSTAGE
A STAGEHAND rushes to Priscilla's mother.
Mrs. Simpson, your kid's on.
Priscilla takes a deep breath, kisses her mother for good luck.
ON STAGE
Priscilla joins Lefty.
Keepin' the ball rollin' is that little girl with the big sense of humor, Miss Priscilla Simpson.
Thanks a million, and I won't settle for a penny less.
(laughing) I love it. They're all yours, kid.
Lefty runs off as the audience applauds. Thanks, Lefty.
Maestro, if you please.
The orchestra strikes up "Whistling in the Dark" and Priscilla begins tap dancing. And, she's no Shirley Temple.

(as she dances) When I got off the train here in Tinseltown, there were a hundred thousand people waiting for me. You don't believe me? Just ask Mae West, she was standing right next to me... You know the Depression hit my home so bad my parents couldn't afford to have my kid brother, so the neighbors had him.
She gets a few titters.
BACKSTAGE
Priscilla's routine continues in the background. approaches Hamilton Brooks and Rochelle. The stagehand
Abernathy, you're next.
Wish me luck, baby? Just win.
As Brooks speaks he smooths out his already-perfectly-smooth white gloves. Applause from the stage.
ON STAGE
Priscilla has finished her act. Lefty takes over at the mic. Priscilla curtseys to hearty applause.
Cute act, sugar.
Lefty, we're staying on you out there in Movieland.
Right you are, Dan. We're coming down to the wire now. And here's Miss
Rochelle Abernathy singing a selection from the opera La Boheme.
DAN FROM CUTOUT (V.O.)
Rochelle glides on stage, poised. mouth tell us she's a pro.
The first notes out of her
Brooks hovers close to the stage, mouthing the aria as his protege sings.
EXT. NEW YORK CITY STREETS - NIGHT
Police SIRENS invade the winter air. Two Motorcycle Cops escort a Yellow Cab at top speed through the whirlwind
activity of Times Square, 42nd Street and Broadway.
The motorcade comes to a screeching halt in front of Radio
Station WPIX.

A banner stretches across the doorway, fluttering in the wind: WELCOME TO DOOLEY'S 1933 ANNUAL AMATEUR CONTEST Before the cab has come to a complete stop, the back door flies open and a fabulous pair of legs step out. CAMERA MOVES UP a white satin gown and we catch a FULL VIEW of AMES PRESCOTT. She sparkles with a smile that's worth a million bucks. A Cop helps Ames out of the taxi.
(to Cops) Thanks, boys, I owe you one.
Ames pulls on her cape and salutes the Cops as if she were tipping a hat. A MAN, hard hit by the times, approaches her.
Ma'am, can you spare two bits for a guy to get a square meal?
I'll raise the ante.
She hands him five bucks.
(handing it back) All I asked for was two bits. It's on me. days!
I got millions.
And she dashes into WPIX.
INT. WPIX - HALLWAY

As Ames rounds a corner she passes a Short Man waddling in a tuxedo, followed by a penguin on a leash. She turns to look at them and collides with her secretary, JOAN. Joan hugs Ames with a sigh of relief. hallway as they talk. They rush down the
Do you have any idea --
-- Yes. I left the Hollywood Contest twelve hours ago. Missed my first plane and the one I caught just landed in Newark. Newark?!
Don't ask. Do you have my speech? running on time? Where's Willis?
(handing her a speech) Here. We're two minutes behind. boss is in the booth. Pacing.
The
Ames reaches out and Joan intuitively hands her a pencil. Ames goes to work on her speech as they continue on their way. They stop just before reaching the ON THE AIR sign flashing over a door.
Did the photographers show up in L.A. yet?
Yeah, I just talked to them.

Okay, call 'em back. Tell them if the winner's there I want those camera's popping. Tell 'em to do it up big. Soon as the phones close, we'll hit them with the new commercial and right before I announce the winner make sure I get a 30-second drum roll.
Ames opens the door and enters the studio. behind. Joan stays close
What new commercial?
Ames fishes into her purse and brings out a cocktail napkin.
Here, I wrote it on the plane. sure everybody gets it. Got it.
Welcome back.

Ames smiles. We're now BACKSTAGE. WPIX is even more chaotic than its sister station in Los Angeles. The contest is coming down to the wire. Ames darts through the zany finalists, but gets caught by a roadblock -- a FAT MAN measuring five by five.
Miss Prescott, you ain't seen nothing
From behind the man, four little Girls pop out. They're decked out like Shirley Temple, and all are doing a bad softshoe to the accompaniment of the fat man's harmonica.
Save it, kids, the public's voting.
She manages to escape and heads toward the...
VOTING STATION

Rows of desks fill a corner of the stage where pretty Girls sit answering phones. As Ames passes each desk, she scoops up piles of tally sheets. She stops and takes out a cigarette. A MAN'S HAND lights it. Ames looks up and sees her boss, WILLIS CUMMINGS, every inch the seasoned ad exec, complete with tux.
Just the man I've been looking for.
You've done it again, Ames. Sponsors happy?
Good. Then I want to talk to you about something.
She leads Willis to a vacant desk. She plops him down in the chair and perches herself on the edge of the desk.
I can't afford to give you another raise.
No, no, no. You just landed the Montgomery account, right? Right. me, have I got a brilliant idea. I'm asking.
Ask
I know Montgomery wants a contest on Coney Island. I have the contest to end all contests! All I need is the Montgomery account.
The PHONE on the desk RINGS. Ames picks it up.
Your vote counts.
She jots down the vote on a piece of paper.
(Southern accent) How ya doin' down there in Mobile?... Sorry to hear that. Do yourself a favor and leave the bum. Dooley's thanks y'all for callin'. She hangs up the phone without missing a beat.
(to Willis) I've already dreamed up 45 contests. Let's face it, you're not going to find anyone better.
Montgomery's a tough customer...
-- I know everyone says he's some sort of crackpot. He's got to be, he just bought Tried & True. And let's face it, it's the worst selling cigarette in the country.
Montgomery's not just some fruitcake, Ames. He's as eccentric as they come. I've never even met the man. He calls all the shots. Not your type at all.
Willis, I'm not going to marry the man. Use me.
Joan interrupts them and hands Ames a stack of papers.
Phones just shut down.
Ames grabs the papers. Joan is off.
The look on Ames' face says "maybe" ain't enough.
We'll talk about it later. Count on it.
Ames looks at the clock on the wall.
Whoa, I'm on in sixty seconds.
She pulls a small pencil from behind her ear and totals the score. Willis tries to give her the slip.
(without looking up) Be seein' you.

Ames finishes her arithmetic and tosses the sheets into a nearby trash can. She then flicks her cigarette in after it. As she walks away, the papers ignite, starting a small fire. A Stagehand rushes in with a fire extinguisher.
INT. WPIX - ON STAGE
The real-life announcer, DAN FRYE, stands next to a cutout of Hollywood's announcer, Lefty McCoy. A Woman in a hula skirt bows to applause and dances offstage.
(to cutout) That about wraps up our talent in New York.
(scratchy) And in Hollywood, Dan. I don't mind tellin you we're kinda antsy waitin' for the big decision.

Ames walks across the stage as the penguin we saw earlier scurries by. His look-alike owner chases after him. Dan doesn't know what to do. With a swoop of her arm, Ames catches the little rascal. The audience is laughing.
Hey shortstuff, where'd ya get the suit?
And now, to bring you the moment we've all been waiting for, Miss Ames Prescott!
(into mic) Good Evening, America. After two weeks of voting, we have our winner. Are you
We hear a DRUM ROLL as we...
INT. KPIX, HOLLYWOOD - ON STAGE

The DRUM ROLL continues over the faces of the anxious finalists. -- Brooks towers behind his sabled opera singer; -- Priscilla snuggles close to her mother; -- The Man with the BELLS shakes nervously causing a RACKET; -- And so on...
Coast to coast, each one of you listening tonight has picked your favorite. On behalf of Dooley's Tooth Powder I'd like to thank all our talented contestants. Now, without any further ado...
We hear the RIPPING of an ENVELOPE.
FANFARE and MUSIC. Priscilla jumps for joy. contestants offer congratulations.
The other
Opera singer Rochelle knows she's in for trouble. She turns to face her mentor Hamilton Brooks. He's gone. And so is her sable. Flashbulbs EXPLODE as Lefty hands Priscilla a check for $1,000.
INT. WILLIS CUMMINGS' NEW YORK PENTHOUSE
Offering an incredible VIEW of the city. delight.
An Art Deco lover's

New York's elite are in the midst of a crazy scavenger hunt, reminding us just how rich the rich are. Ames stands over a baby grand piano where a group (including Willis) have gathered around and are singing "Makin' Whoopee". CARLOTTA CUMMINGS, Willis' wife, is running around doing her best to sort out the items already brought in. She has a flamboyant personality, accented by the feather boa she drags along with her.
Please! I must have your attention. Be still, please!
The guests quiet down. The singing stops.

Now then, we've already collected a live monkey, a New York Times from Black Thursday, and a life preserver from the Staten Island Ferry. The final item of the evening is to find a man with five hundred dollars on his person. Dead or alive?
And please stay out of trouble. Remember, you must be back by sunrise. (MORE)
Do keep dry, it looks like rain. To the victor belong the spoils! Au revoir, children. Have fun.
Most of the guests dash out of the apartment. remain at the piano. Ames and Willis
Don't tell me you're going along with this lunacy?
Who wouldn't want to meet a man with five hundred dollars? You never know, I might even dig up the elusive Mr. Montgomery.
Ames grabs her purse and heads for the door. beat and turns back to Willis. She pauses for a
Don't forget, boss, we've got some unfinished business. Happy hunting.
Ames gestures with a quick salute and is out the door.
EXT. NEW YORK PIER
FOG HORNS break the silence of the cold night air. is deserted.
The pier
A taxi pulls up in front of a dilapidated warehouse. steps out.
(to the Driver) Can you wait?
He speeds away.
Guess you can't.
Ames looks around her and then proceeds into the...
Ames INT. WAREHOUSE - NIGHT

Moonlight streams in through broken glass. dusty floor. Ames makes her way through the cobwebs. where she's going. Crates litter the She seems to know She stops in front of a hefty pile of crates and pushes them aside. A bulletproof, raid-proof, and soundproof metal door stares her right in the face. Ames presses a BUZZER. A peephole in the door slides open. She RECOGNIZES a pair of eyes.
'Evening, Teddy. The massive door swings open.
elegant underworld...
Ames enters New York's most
INT. CASINO
Dealers nod hello. Friendly faces smile at her. Ames approaches ZIGGY, one of the bouncers. His bashed-in nose and cauliflower ears don't quite jibe with his tuxedo.
How goes it, Ziggy?
Not good, not bad. Where you been keeping yourself? Around.
Any hot rollers tonight?
Depends what you're looking for. I'm out to win a scavenger hunt.
You might try the roulette tables. Gotcha.
Don't take any brass knuckles.
Ames passes on her salute and makes her way through the crowd. A garish fellow by the name of CHIC grabs her.
Boy, oh boy, what's a high-class mama like you doin' out on a night like this?
Looking for some action. can find any? Know where I
You certainly picked the right spot. And Chic here's just the right guy to show you a hot time. Yes sir, I could go for a doll like you. No kiddin', you're just my type.
Ames snuggles closer to him, and in her sexiest voice...
Got five hundred big ones, Chic?
Five hundred! Oh, you mean... Say, what d'ya take me for, a millionaire?
Too bad. We coulda had a good time, if you know what I mean. No kiddin'.

Ames struts off leaving Chic with his mouth hanging open. She stops at a roulette table. SPOTS a nice-looking Gent with stacks of chips. She is about to make her move when the Gent opens his mouth and he's toothless. Ames quickly changes her mind. She makes her way through the tables. Checks out another roulette game. A Fat Woman places a bet and then backs away from the table, revealing BENNETT SLOANE, handsome, debonair, and losing. He places a pile of chips on 31-Black. Ames squeezes in next to him. Bad move.
AMES (Cont'd)
He turns and looks at her.
I beg your pardon?
The wheel spins.
Number seventeen, Red. Bennett loses.
He keeps his chips on the Black.
Play it on the Red. What if I lose?
What d'ya think you been doing? me. Trust
With the ease of a pro she takes his remaining chips and places them on "The Red." She winks at the CROUPIER. He spins the wheel.
The number is nine, Red.
Congratulations.
Before Bennett even knows what's happening, Ames scoops up his winnings and hands them to him.
How much you think you got there?
About seven hundred.
Guess this is your lucky night. Must be.
She slips her arm through his as they saunter to the Cashier. Bennett cashes in his chips. Fifty-fifty.
He hands her a bundle of cash.
I wouldn't dream of it.
At least let me buy you a drink.
They're off to the bar. Find two empty stools.
What are you drinking?
(to Bartender) Martini, very dry. Make it two.
Their drinks are delivered.
Happy days!
They clink glasses and sip.
I'm Bennett Sloane.
He extends his hand. Ames shakes it.
How do you do, Bennett Sloane. Prescott.
Bennett doesn't let go of her hand, nor can he take his eyes off her.
Oh, excuse me. It's just that I've been away and you're the first pretty face I've seen in awhile.
Aw, that's sweet. Where you been, the North Pole?
(shocked) How did you know?
You on the level?
I was on an expedition with Admiral Byrd. I'm a zoologist.
Fascinating profession. before. Never met one
Everyone says that.
I hate to sound like everyone.
Believe me, you're not like anyone I've ever met.
Thanks, I guess.
Funny our meeting like this. Maybe fate. Who knows? Uh-oh.
Drink fast, Mr. Zoologist.
Ames' eyes shift to the rear of the casino. crash into the joint.
Alright, everyone! This is a raid! Hit the floor!
The Patrons scream and run in all directions. Bennett and Ames jump to their feet. Ames pulls Bennett out toward the front. No, this way...
He guides her out a side window where it's pouring rain.
INT. CHAUFFEUR-DRIVEN BENTLEY (TRAVELING)
Ames and Bennett ride in the back seat, soaked from the rain. They dry themselves with towels.
Great getaway car.
BATES, the chauffeur, addresses Bennett, his eyes looking in the REAR VIEW MIRROR.
VOICE
Bennett turns to Ames. Where to?
Wait a minute. (points to Bates) One... (points to Bennett) Two...
Bennett reaches over the front seat and lifts up a birdcage with an exotic bird in it.
You really are a zoologist.
This is Otto, an Arctic tern. Very rare. They're only found in the Arctic region of the Beaufort Sea.
Bennett puts the cage back down. How does he like New York?
He loves the nightlife. Where to?
(laughing) 200 Riverside Drive.
Got that, Bates?
Yes, Mr. Sloane.
Ames rifles through her purse looking for something.
What do you need? Cigarette.
AMES
Bennett pushes a button and a portable bar opens up in front of them. It's well-stocked with booze. Sitting prominently is a goblet filled with cigarettes. Ames helps herself and NOTICES the brand.
Didn't know anyone still
Oh, I don't smoke.
He lights her cigarette. The Bentley slows down, pulling up to a curb.

You're a gambling zoologist who doesn't know how to gamble. You drive around in a chauffeured Bentley with an Arctic tern, and you're well-stocked with cigarettes you don't even smoke. I don't get you.
(shrugs) Wait'll you really get to know me.
Bates opens the back door and they climb out.
This your place?
No, we're going to a party.
Well, uh, I really don't...
-- Sure, you do.
She smiles that smile and he can't refuse.
INT. CUMMINGS' PENTHOUSE
The party is still in full swing, except that a few weary guests have passed out in various corners. Carlotta is judging the hunt, counting the cash of a Man found by two of her hunters.
So sorry, you're two dollars short.
The Man empties his pockets hoping to come up with the rest.
walk right into the bedlam.
Looks like the breadline's already started to form this morning.
Ames frowns at his remark and grabs Bennett's arm when a sultry BRUNETTE glides past them.
Bennett, I heard you were back. the Eskimos treat you? Grand.
Ames stops in her tracks.
Shoots Bennett a look.
An old army buddy.
She leads Bennett toward Carlotta.
Carlotta! Carlotta! I've got a man! I've won! (to Bennett) Oh, I forgot to tell you. This is a scavenger hunt.
Before he can answer, Carlotta spots them.
What did you say about a man, dear?
I've got the man.
We all think we've had the man at one time or another. But I must admit, this one does come close. (to Bennett) Hello, you naughty boy. I thought you couldn't make it. C'est la vie.
(to Ames) Oh, I forgot to tell you. Small world. Isn't it?
Ames, dear, Willis has been looking for you.
Ames looks around. Where is he?
He's here somewhere. Carlotta drags Bennett off.
(to Bennett) Don't go away.
Ames weaves through the crowd looking for Willis. a staircase and goes upstairs. She reaches
IN THE HALLWAY

Ames goes door to door looking for Willis. No sign of him. She reaches the last door and bolts in. It's dark. She flicks on the light. Willis pops up in bed. He looks different. He's bald. Willis' hairpiece covers a dummy head on the night table next to him.
Willis, is that you? I hardly recognized you.
Willis automatically pats his head. Mum's the word.
She sits on the edge of the bed.
Carlotta said you were looking for me.
I was, about three hours ago. What's up?
You got the job.
She throws her arms around him.
You won't be sorry, Willis. I'll turn the name Tried & True into a household word. Fine.
You do that. 'Night.

She kisses him on the cheek, tucks him in. in his ears and turns over. He sticks earplugs Ames clicks off the light, walks out, shutting the door. Darkness. Light fills the room as the door flies open. silhouette in the doorway. The light snaps on. Ames'
How come everybody knows Bennett Sloane? Willis hasn't budged. one of his earplugs.
Ames crosses over to him and pulls out
(shouting) How come everybody knows Bennett Sloane?
Ames, darling, please. It's five o'clock in the morning. Don't play games with me.
(shakes the bed) Tell me. Tried & True.
What about Tried & True? His father founded it.
This is very interesting.
She sits down on the bed next to Willis.
Hand me a sleeping pill.
Ames reaches for a bottle of pills on the night table next to her. Willis takes one with a glass of water.
Come on, come on. Give.
When the market crashed Bennett's father just about lost his shirt. That's where Montgomery came in? That's it.
Now, good night.
He turns off the lamp next to him. next to Ames flashes on. Pitch black. The lamp
Montgomery came in and what? Bought enough of the company to take control. Satisfied? For now.
Sweet dreams.
Ames hops off the bed, flicks off the light.
Ames, you in here?
The lights zip on again. Bennett stands in the doorway holding a trophy. Willis' head disappears under his pillow.
Congratulations.
He hands her the trophy.
Why didn't you tell me you're Tried & True?
I'm not. My father was. (as Willis muffles a GROAN) Willis, how've you been?
He's a little tired. Nothing a good night's sleep won't cure.
Willis' hand slips out from under the covers and fumbles with his lamp. Finally, darkness.
INT. HALLWAY
Outside of Willis' bedroom, Ames and Bennett saunter down the hallway.
What were you and Willis doing? Business. Whose? Yours. Mine?
AMES
I'm going to be running the contest for Tried & True.
Then you must work for Willis.
I'm his right-hand man. Looks like we'll be seeing a lot of each other.
You, me and J. Everett Montgomery.
No one sees a lot of J. Everett Montgomery.
Maybe you can tell me why no one sees him. I wish I knew.
Bennett stops and turns to Ames.
Remember when I told you I was in the Arctic?
It's not something you forget.

Well, the reason I came home was because my father died and left me President of the company. And that's not exactly a great job for a zoologist. This Montgomery fellow is running things and I can't get to him.
You mean you haven't met him yet?
No, and to tell the truth, it worries me. He didn't even consult me when he decided to put on a contest.
That's one thing you don't have to worry about. Contests are my specialty. Trust me. On that line we...
EXT. TRAIN STATION PLATFORM - HOLLYWOOD - DAY

Two trains line the tracks. The platform is crowded with boarding passengers. Redcaps, baggage carts, lots of pushing and shoving. Priscilla Simpson, the winner of the amateur contest, stands with her mother. But something looks fishy. Priscilla seems less than angelic. Much less. She wears a schoolboy cap over her tousled hair. She has all the moves of a red-blooded American hustler. And her mother doesn't look so kosher, either.
Here you go, Mae.
Priscilla hands her a small roll of bills.
You play a great mother.
Thanks, cookie, don't spread it around.

is lifted over heads in the crowd. Following close behind is its owner, Hamilton Brooks, the dapper gent from the talent contest. Trailing him is the sable coat, now covering a luscious-looking REDHEAD. Brooks taps a PORTER with the tip of his gold cane. Pardon me.
Where are you taking that
No, you're not. Put it in the compartment next to mine.
But, sir, it's occupied.

Brooks carefully peels off one of his white gloves, entrusting it to the redhead. His bare hand reaches into his pocket and brings out a $10 bill. He stuffs it into the porter's hand. The porter whistles through his fingers. The bike pauses midair. The porter signals to the guy carrying it. The bike does an about-face. Excellent.
kissing goodbye. Your attention, please. The 20th Century Limited is now boarding at
track eight for Albuquerque, Dodge City, Tulsa, New Orleans, Chattanooga,
That's me... Happy days.
Priscilla disappears into the crowd. A NUN stands on the steps of Priscilla's train counting heads as a group of children pass her single-file boarding the train.
And Priscilla slips in line.
INT. TRAIN (A FEW MINUTES LATER)

Priscilla moves down the narrow aisle clutching a suitcase. Hamilton Brooks and the redhead head toward her from the other end of the car. The redhead is struggling with Brooks' bags. Priscilla RECOGNIZES him and pulls her cap down over her eyes as she squeezes past him. The train jolts forward, throwing Priscilla against Brooks. 'Scuse me, Pop.
I dare say I'm not your Pop.
His cane snaps in front of her.
She can't get by.
Ever been to Cherokee Springs, Louisiana? Never.
Potterville, P.A.? Certainly not.
Guess we never met.
Priscilla touches the sable.
PRISCILLA (Cont'd)

She hurries off. CLOSEUP - A CIGARETTE CASE The initials H.B. engraved on it. CAMERA PULLS BACK to reveal Priscilla's slippery little hand around it. She tucks it into her pants as she reaches the next car.
EXT. ROOF OF TRAIN

Priscilla leaps across each speeding car of the train, heading for the caboose. The train WHISTLE BLASTS warning her of something ahead. Priscilla whips around facing a rickety bridge just high enough to let the train pass through. She springs off the train and clutches onto the bridge, her feet dangling in the air. The train roars by below. The wooden bridge CRACKS and SPLITS. The chunk Priscilla hangs onto lingers by a thread. It swings her into the air, positioning her next to an open boxcar. She dives in and slides right into a gang of Tramps playing poker.
Deal me in, boys.
EXT. RURAL ROAD - TULSA, OKLAHOMA

Dust-covered. A scattering of clapboard HOUSES CREAK in the HOWLING WIND, facing acres of dried cornstalks. Tumbleweeds whirl by. A tractor rolls THROUGH THE FRAME. As it lazily moves along, we see Priscilla. She's carrying her suitcase and wearing a bandanna over her face. The rest of her is covered in dust. Priscilla passes TWO tired-looking WOMEN sitting on a front porch surrounded by a pack of grubby-looking children.
Howdy, stranger! Haven't seen you in awhile.
Just blew in from Hollywood.
Ain't fittin' for a girl with no rightful parents to go wandrin' 'round way you do.
See y'all doin' fine. Been home yet?
Headin' there now.
You better git...
(to 1st woman) Hush up. (to Priscilla) Why don't you run on home now, honey bunch.
EXT. ROAD - A BULLDOZER
crushes into the side of a clapboard house. Onlookers sadly watch the destruction. Priscilla struts down the road. takes off toward it.
A few ragged
She spots the wreckage and When she reaches the crumbling house she finds her older brother BILL standing there watching.
(out of breath) Holy Moses, Bill, what's going on!?
We tried to reach you, hon. Government bought up every house for two square miles. They're putting in a highway.

He and Priscilla walk down the road to a jalopy packed with furniture. Bill's wife CLAIRE is tying on a mattress. She and Priscilla hug. A BABY CRIES from a cradle on the truck; Priscilla leans over and kisses him.
Where we gonna live? With my folks.
That ought to be cozy.
Got any other suggestions?
Matter of fact, I have.
She reaches into her pants pocket and takes out a wad of bills. Hands it to him.
I don't like taking money from my kid sister.
